nodes.title: Return a shallow copy at depth 0
nodes.why: Depth 0 means no flattening, but we still return a new array to avoid mutating the original.
nodes.tradeoff: Copying is safer, but creates one extra array allocation.

nodes.title: Flatten nested arrays by reducing depth
nodes.why: Each recursive call removes one nesting level until depth reaches 0.
nodes.alternative: Use an iterative stack instead of recursion.
nodes.tradeoff: Recursion is easy to read, but very deep inputs can hit call-stack limits.

nodes.title: Keep non-array values as they are
nodes.why: Only arrays should be flattened. Primitives and objects should keep their position.
nodes.alternative: Treat all iterable values as flattenable.
nodes.tradeoff: Strict array-only behavior is predictable, but less generic for custom iterable rules.

nodes.title: Stop early when no flattening is needed
nodes.why: This keeps depth semantics clear and avoids unnecessary reduce work.
nodes.alternative: Let reduce run even when depth is 0.
nodes.tradeoff: Early return is faster and clearer, with minimal extra branching.

nodes.title: Use reduce to build the output array
nodes.why: Reduce gives a compact one-pass way to accumulate flattened values.
nodes.tradeoff: Shorter code, but some developers find loops easier to debug.

nodes.title: Concat either recursive flatten or raw element
nodes.why: This line handles both cases in one expression: nested arrays recurse, others append directly.
nodes.alternative: Split into an `if/else` block for readability.
nodes.tradeoff: Compact expression is concise, but longer ternaries can be harder to scan.

nodes.title: Seed stack in reverse to preserve output order
nodes.why: Stack is LIFO, so reversing at push time keeps final processing left-to-right.
nodes.alternative: Push left-to-right, then reverse final result at the end.
nodes.tradeoff: Order stays correct without a final reverse, but the push logic is less intuitive at first glance.

nodes.title: Use `[value, depth]` pairs to control flatten level
nodes.why: Tracking depth per stack item lets the loop mimic recursion safely.
nodes.alternative: Use true recursion and pass `depth - 1` in function calls.
nodes.tradeoff: Iterative approach avoids call-stack overflow, but needs extra bookkeeping.

nodes.title: Append terminal values into result
nodes.why: When value is not flattenable at current depth, it belongs in final output.
nodes.alternative: Buffer per-level outputs and merge later.
nodes.tradeoff: Direct append is simple and fast, but requires careful ordering logic earlier.
